title: Cherry jewellery stand
subtitle: A turned-peg jewellery stand in cherry wood with a circular base
excerpt: A free-standing jewellery stand from English cherry: a circular base 200 mm across, an upright post 180 mm tall, and six peg rods for hanging necklaces and bracelets. The pegs are dowels turned or cut to a slight taper and glued into bored holes. An intermediate seasoned-wood project.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the peg-stand construction method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the bedroom jewellery and trinket stand.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber dried to equilibrium moisture content. Cherry is a fine-grained hardwood that planes and sands to a silk-smooth finish.
A cylindrical rod of wood used here as the peg arms of the jewellery stand. Available in 8 to 10 mm diameters from timber suppliers.
The direction of wood fibres. Cherry sands beautifully along the grain; across-grain scratches are especially visible on this fine-textured wood.
A small guide hole drilled before a larger hole to ensure accurate positioning and prevent the wood from splitting.

content.text: English cherry finishes beautifully with a single coat of Danish oil. The surface takes on a warm amber colour that deepens over years. A jewellery stand needs accurate hole-boring rather than complex joinery:
pegs are glued into 8 mm holes bored in the upright post at regular angles.
cherry is sold by specialist timber merchants in small off-cuts. A piece 250 mm by 250 mm by 25 mm covers all parts for this project.
From 25 mm cherry: one circular base 200 mm diameter; one upright post 50 mm by 50 mm by 180 mm. From 8 mm cherry or birch
rod: six peg arms, each 80 mm long. The base circle can be marked with a compass and then sawn with a coping saw or jigsaw; it does not need to be perfectly round.

Saw the base circle and sand the edges smooth with 320-grit on the sanding block. Mark the centre of the base with the marking gauge and bore an 8 mm hole 15 mm deep into the base face for the post tenon. The post bottom has a 40 mm long by 8 mm round tenon turned or carefully pared with the chisel; this tenon glues into the base hole. Check the post stands vertical by testing it against a try square on a flat surface.

positions on the post in two rows of three: one row at 60 mm from the base and one at 120 mm. Space each row at 120-degree intervals around the post so the stand looks balanced from any direction. Bore each hole at 8 mm diameter and 15 mm deep, angled 20 degrees upward so the pegs slope up and items stay on.
Apply a drop of PVA glue into each peg hole and push the
pegs home. Glue the post tenon into the base hole. Allow to dry fully. Sand all surfaces with 320-grit along the
direction. Apply one coat of Danish oil, wipe off after ten minutes, and allow four hours to dry. Cherry responds well to a second oil coat applied after twelve hours.

attrs.symptom: The post is not vertical after gluing
attrs.cause: Base hole not bored perfectly perpendicular
attrs.fix: Before the glue sets, pack one side of the base hole gap with a sliver of the same wood, pushing the post to vertical. Allow to dry with the post held against a square. If dry, cut the post tenon and re-bore the hole.

attrs.symptom: A peg pulled out under a heavy necklace
attrs.cause: Glue joint insufficient or hole too loose
attrs.fix: Drill out the old hole 1 mm larger. Cut a replacement peg from a slightly larger dowel to create a snug fit. Re-glue.
